v.t. To bring into operation or use, to make use of, to apply
adj. (f. -ī), Of work, working, busy (e.g. kām-kā din); of use, useful, of service, serviceable, of importance (to, -se); of need

To do work, to be busy; to act; to engage in practical affairs; to mind (one's own, apnā) business; to accomplish a business; to act or operate (upon, -par), to be of use (to), to take effect (on), be effectual, to succeed; to settle the business (of, -kā), to kill, destroy
To begin a work, to take or put a work in hand
To take in work, to undertake a job; to take over charge of an office (from, -se); to work (one, or a beast, &c.), to make use (of, -se), to use
To obtain an employment, or a post, &c.
To shirk work, to skulk; to work in a dilatory manner

(-se), Work to be got out (of); things to be gained, or to result
adj. Without work, unemployed, unoccupied; vacant; unused, lying idle.

ety: Pehl. kamar; Zend kamara, fr. kamar = S. kmar
def: s.f. The loins, the waist; a girdle, zone, belt; the middle (of a mountain, &c.); the flank (of an army); an arch
subs: (-par), To gird up the loins (for), to get ready, prepare, arm (for); to resolve, to be intent (on)
adj. Having the loins girt, in a state of readiness, on the alert, ready for action, prepared, armed
adj. = kamar-basta; — s.m. A girdle, zone, sash, belt, waistband; a long piece of cloth folded round the loins; the string with which drawers, &c. are fastened (syn. izār-band)
(-kī), To encourage, inspirit, give hope of success (to), to stimulate (one to persevere)
s.f. Girding up the loins, preparing, getting ready; preparation (for action), prepared state, alertness
s.m. A zone, or cincture (of gold or silver); — a tie-beam
'To rise holding the loins'; to be in a weak or feeble state
or kamar thāmnā (-kī), To seize (one) by the waist; to urge a claim (against one); — to support, back, help, encourage
adj. (f. -ī), Gone in the loins; impotent; — hump-backed
(-kī), 'The loins or back to be broken'; to be deserted by (one's) friends; to give way or sink under the loss of a child, &c.; to be deprived of hope, to lose confidence or courage
(-kī), To break the back (of); to take away all hope (from), to discourage, &c.; to alienate one's friends or adherents
The loins or back to give way; to have a pain in the loins (from long standing); the back to become palsied
To stretch one self out for brief repose, to lie down
or kamar kasnā = kamar bāṅdhnā, q.v.

s.f. Ungirding the loins; undoing or opening the waistband; (Mil.) the order to take off accoutrements; — a fee levied by a government peon from a person over whom he is placed in charge for permission to perform the common functions of life
s.m. The part of a beam which projects outside a wall
To undo or throw off (one's) belt, &c.; to quit service; to take (one's) ease
(-ko), To have a sore back (a horse); to get a back-ache (through lying too long in bed)
(-kī), To strike sideways; to attack (an army) in flank
To take courage or heart; to undertake a work of expense
'To shake or move the loins'; to endeavour; — to copulate.
wety: Borrowed from Classical Persian کَمَر (kamar). First attested in c. 1635 as Middle Hindi کمر (kmr /⁠kamar⁠/). Displaced Middle Hindi لنک (lãk, “waist”).

def: s.m. = kamarā, q.v. s.m. A room, chamber, an apartment; cabin (of a ship).

wety: Borrowed from Portuguese câmara, from Old Galician-Portuguese camara, from Latin camera.

def: s.m. A room, chamber, an apartment; cabin (of a ship).

adj. & s.m. Taking any shape at will; beautiful, pleasing ( = kām-rūpin, or kām-rūpī); — name of a district lying to the east of Bengal, the western portion of Assam

def: adj. & s.m. See s.v. kām. s.m. Action, act, deed, work, doing, handiwork, performance; work, labour, duty, task, job; business, occupation, employment, office, function; operation, undertaking, transaction, affair, matter, thing, concern, interest; — a hard task, a difficult matter; a feat; object, end, purport; — workmanship; manufacture; needlework, embroidery; — service, use; serviceableness, fitness (for); need (of, -se), occasion (for); — the mail-packet, mails, post-bag
subs: adj. Zealously following or pursuing a business; occupied, busy

v.n. To come into use; to come of use (to, -ke), be of use or service, prove serviceable (to); to be of avail, to avail, to stand in stead; to be wanted; to be used, or consumed, or spent (in, -meṅ); to fall, be killed or slain (in battle, &c.)
(-kā, or apnā), To increase the work (of); — to lay aside work, to cease from, or give over, work
(-kā), To spoil or mar the work (of); to make a mess of a business, &c.; to put a spoke in (one's) wheel; to baffle, to foil; to ruin (one's) reputation or credit
v.n. A work to be begun; a workshop to be opened, a business to be started or established
v.n. To go to work; to go on duty
v.n. To be at work; to be engaged in work, to have a post or employment, be in office
v.n. To come or be of use, to prove of service ( = kām ānā); to have to do (with, -se), to have business (with), to require, be in need (of), have occasion (for)
(or -par) lagānā (-ko), To set (one) to work, give (one) work to do; to give (one) an employment, to employ, take into service
(-kā), To finish a work or business, accomplish a task; to settle the business (of), to put an end (to one), to despatch, to kill
(-kā), A work or task to be finished, &c.; the business (of a person) to be settled, to be put an end (to), to be killed
(-kā), To put a work or job in hand; to set a (machine, &c.) to work
(apnā), To keep a work going; to further or advance a work or business; to manage to do (with, -meṅ)
adj. Sufficient to go on with; answering as a make-shift; serviceable
s.m. One who shirks work; one who does not give his full tale of work; a skulker, an idle fellow
adj. Worked (with, -kā); having any particular kind of work; embroidered; — s.m. Man of business, manager, agent, steward
s.f. A kind of embroidery work on net or muslin (small flowers worked in gold or silver thread)
(-kā), To inspect the work (of), to overlook, supervise
(-ko), To give work or employment (to), to employ; to make over office or charge (to); to render good service (to, -kā), to work well (for)
(-se), To have business or work (with), to have to do (with), to occupy oneself (with); to be connected (with)
(-ko), To give (one) work to do; to make over charge of an office ( = kām denā)
v.n. To be or become unfit for use, to become unserviceable, be past work; to be removed from office or employment
v.t. To bring into operation or use, to make use of, to apply
adj. (f. -ī), Of work, working, busy (e.g. kām-kā din); of use, useful, of service, serviceable, of importance (to, -se); of need
adj. & s.m. Laborious, industrious, busy, active; managing, superintending; — a worker, laborious or industrious man; manager, superintendent

To do work, to be busy; to act; to engage in practical affairs; to mind (one's own, apnā) business; to accomplish a business; to act or operate (upon, -par), to be of use (to), to take effect (on), be effectual, to succeed; to settle the business (of, -kā), to kill, destroy
To begin a work, to take or put a work in hand
To take in work, to undertake a job; to take over charge of an office (from, -se); to work (one, or a beast, &c.), to make use (of, -se), to use
To obtain an employment, or a post, &c.
To shirk work, to skulk; to work in a dilatory manner
To kill two birds with one stone
(-ko), To bring into use, to use, to employ; to apply; to expend
(-se), To carry into effect, to effect (one's) purpose (by means of), to accomplish (one's) desire; to get (one's) work done
(-se), Work to be got out (of); things to be gained, or to result
adj. Without work, unemployed, unoccupied; vacant; unused, lying idle.
